elements.sent-2411-001: In the last years, Regev's reduction has been used as a quantum algorithmic tool for providing a quantum advantage for variants of the decoding problem.
elements.sent-2411-002: Following this line of work, the authors of \cite{JSW+24 have recently come up with a quantum algorithm called ``decoded quantum interferometry'' that is able to solve in polynomial time several optimization problems.}
elements.sent-2411-003: They study in particular the Optimal Polynomial Interpolation (OPI) problem, which can be seen as a decoding problem on Reed-Solomon codes.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we provide strong improvements for some instantiations of the OPI problem.
elements.sent-2411-005: The most notable improvements are for the $\ISIS_{\infty}$ problem (originating from lattice-based cryptography) on Reed-Solomon codes but we also study different constraints for OPI.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results provide natural and convincing decoding problems for which we believe to have a quantum advantage.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our proof techniques involve the use of a soft decoder for Reed-Solomon codes, namely the decoding algorithm from Koetter and Vardy~\cite{KV03 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In order to be able to use this decoder in the setting of Regev's reduction, we provide a novel generic reduction from a syndrome decoding problem to a coset sampling problem, providing a powerful and simple to use theorem, which generalizes previous work and is of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also provide an extensive study of OPI using the Koetter and Vardy algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-010: In the last years, Regev's reduction has been used as a quantum algorithmic tool for providing a quantum advantages for variants of decoding problems.
elements.sent-2411-011: Chen, Liu and Zhandry~\cite{CLZ22 initiated this line of research and showed how to use Regev's reduction for algorithmic purposes.}
elements.sent-2411-012: They solved in quantum polynomial time the $\SIS_{\infty}$ problem for some parameters.
elements.sent-2411-013: While the parameters where this quantum algorithm works in polynomial time are somewhat extreme, there is no known classical algorithm that solves $\SIS_{\infty}$ for these parameters.
elements.sent-2411-014: Then, Yamakawa and Zhandry~\cite{YZ24 proved their algorithm for quantum advantage without structure using essentially a decoding problem.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Recently, \cite{JSW+24 has come up with a quantum algorithm called ``decoded quantum interferometry'' that is able to solve in polynomial time several optimization problems.}
elements.sent-2411-016: One of them is sparse max-XORSAT and the other one is Optimal Polynomial Interpolation (OPI).
elements.sent-2411-017: The quantum algorithm achieves on certain instances of the first problem a better approximation ratio on average than simulated annealing.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, there are specialized classical algorithms tailored to this problem which provide a better approximation ratio.
elements.sent-2411-019: Much more intriguing is the second problem, namely OPI.
elements.sent-2411-020: It basically consists for a finite field $\Fq$ in finding a polynomial in $\Fq[X]$ of some degree less than some bound $k$ which satisfies as many intersection constraints as possible.
elements.sent-2411-021: Here there are as many intersection constraints as there are non zero elements $x$ in $\Fq$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Each constraint corresponds to a set $\cS_x \subset \Fq$ and the intersection constraint at $x$ just means that $P(x)$ should be in $\cS_x$.
elements.sent-2411-023: To illustrate the quantum speedup which can be achieved, the authors of \cite{JSW+24 consider the case of balanced sets (the size of the $\cS_x$'s is $\approx \frac{q {2}$), the bound on the degree is $k = \frac{q {10}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this case, their quantum algorithm is able to produce in polynomial time polynomials of degree $\leq d$ which satisfy a fraction $\frac{1 {2 + \frac{\sqrt{19 {20 \approx 0.7179$ of constraints.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Remarkably the best known polynomial time classical algorithm achieves to satisfy only a fraction $0.55$ of the constraints.
elements.sent-2411-026: As the authors note in \cite{JSW+24 , the margin of the quantum advantage for the approximation fraction is satisfyingly large.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Both problems are actually an instance of a decoding problem which appears in rate distortion theory.
elements.sent-2411-028: We are given a linear code $\C$, \ie\ a subspace of $\F_q^n$ of some dimension $k$ and a word $\yv=(y_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n \in \Fqn$ and we are asked to find a codeword $\cv=(c_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ (an element of $\C$) which is sufficiently close to $\yv$, where the distance $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is say some additive function $d(\yv,\cv) = \sum_{i=1 ^n f_i(c_i,y_i)$, where the $f_i$'s are some suitable nonnegative functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: This problem is generally solved (and interesting) for parameters $k$ and bound $D$ on the distance $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ for which there might be even exponentially many codewords meeting the distance bound, but this does not make the problem necessarily easy.
elements.sent-2411-030: Right now, there are only very special classes of codes for which there is a satisfying solution to this problem such as for instance convolutional codes, LDGM codes or polar codes.
elements.sent-2411-031: Interestingly enough, decoded quantum interferometry solves this kind of problem by using the quantum Fourier transform to transform a (classical) solver of the decoding problem in the injective regime into a solver in the surjective regime.
elements.sent-2411-032: Here by injective regime, we mean that the distance bound is so strong, that for a given $\yv$ there is typically at most one solution of the decoding problem, namely at most a unique $\cv \in \C$ such that $d(\yv,\cv) \leq D$.
elements.sent-2411-033: This corresponds to the more traditional decoding problem appearing in error correction where we are given a noisy codeword $\cv + \ev$, where $\cv \in \C$ and $\ev$ is a ``small'' error vector in $\F_q^n$, where we want a decoding algorithm $\aa_{dec}$ which given as input $\cv + \ev$ is able to recover the close codeword $\cv$.
elements.sent-2411-034: We are in this case typically in the regime where it is only $\cv$ which is close enough to $\cv + \ev$.
elements.sent-2411-035: It turns out that this way of turning a classical algorithm solving the decoding problem in the injective regime into a quantum algorithm solving the decoding problem in the surjective regime is precisely what Regev has achieved in lattice based cryptography when he reduced quantumly the SIS problem to the LWE problem.
elements.sent-2411-036: To explain Regev's reduction \cite{R05 in coding theoretic terms, let us bring the notion of {\em dual code .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The dual $\C^\bot$ of a linear code $\C \subset \F_q^n$ is a subspace of $\F_q^n$ defined as \begin{equation}\C^{\bot} = \{\yv \in \F_q^n : \yv \cdot \cv = 0 for each \cv \in \C\}\end{equation} where $\yv \cdot \cv$ is the canonical inner product of these vectors in $\F_q^n$.
elements.sent-2411-038: We fix a function $f : \F_q^n \rightarrow \mathbb{C}$ with $\norm{f _2 = 1$ so that $|f|^2$ will serve as a probability distribution of the errors we feed in the decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Regev's reduction consists in performing the following operations: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\sqrt{|\C|}} \sum_{\cv \in \C, \ev \in \F_q^n} f(\ev) \ket{\cv + \ev}\ket{\ev} \xrightarrow{Decoder} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\C|}} \sum_{\cv \in \C, \ev \in \F_q^n} f(\ev) \ket{\cv + \ev} \xrightarrow{QFT_{\F_q^n}} \frac{q^n}{\sqrt{|\C|}} \sum_{\yv \in \C^\bot} \hf(\yv)\ket{\yv},\end{equation} where $QFT_{\F_q^n}$ denotes the quantum Fourier transform with respect to $\F_q^n$.
